q: According to Newton's second law, an object that is accelerating must have a net force acting on it.
An object moving in a circle, such as a ball on the end of a string, must therefore have a force applied to it to keep it moving in a straight line.
In the absence of this force, the object would not move along a circle but move in a straight line, according to Newton's first law.
For an object to move in a circle, a force is needed to deviate from its straight line path and accelerate towards the centre of the circle.
The tension in the string provides the required centrifugal force for the ball to keep moving in circular path.
There are different forces which can provide centripetal force depending on the motion of bodies under various conditions.
When whirling a body attached to a string, a tension force is originated.
An attractive force exists between the sun and the earth in a direction parallel to the path of the earth.
The Coulomb attractive force between the nucleus and the electron acts as the required centripetal force for the electron to move around the nucleus.
When a car turns in a circular path or a curve, a frictional force exists between the road and the car tires.
This frictional force acts normally to the direction of motion of the car, causing the car to move in a straight path.
When a car moves in a circular path which is inclined to the horizontal, the reaction force of the road acts normally to the car.
The horizontal component of normal force is the required centrifugal force.
The upward thrust (lifting force) acts normally to an aeroplane body.
On a flat road (unbanked road), this force is supplied by friction between the tires and the road.
If the frictional force is not sufficient, such as under wet and slippery conditions, the car will skid out of the circular path.
Banking of roads is raising the inner edge of the curved road higher than the outer edge.
The angle that the inclined track makes with horizontal is called angle of banking.
The normal force $F_{N}$ exerted by a banked road is perpendicular to the road.
For every banked curve, there is a limiting speed at which the entire centripetal force is supplied by the horizontal component of the normal force.
